one_line_takeaway: Moving a malicious query's harmful keyword from text into a Stable-Diffusion or typography image reliably jailbreaks safety-aligned open-source MLLMs (ASR ~72% on LLaVA-1.5), and a simple prepended safety prompt is a strong training-free mitigation.
key_finding: Typographic and SD+Typography images dramatically raise jailbreak success over a text-only baseline. On LLaVA-1.5-7B average ASR rises from 41.01 (text-only) to 71.52 with typography (+30.51) and 72.14 with SD+Typography (+31.13); on LLaVA-1.5-13B from 44.55 to 72.91 (+28.36). SD-only images help far less (45.37, +4.36). Extreme cases: Illegal Activity on LLaVA-7B goes 5.25 -> 79.38 (typo); Financial Advice and Health Consultation reach ~100. The safety prompt cuts LLaVA-1.5-7B ASR from 77.33 to 15.68 (tiny) and 72.14 to 17.08 (default average), beating the cited MLLM-Protector (72.14 -> 26.11). Ablation: the larger/more instruction-capable backbone is MORE vulnerable (Vicuna-7B 77.33 vs Phi-2-2.7B 66.72).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) an empirical observation that multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) can be jailbroken by pairing a malicious text query with a *query-relevant* image, even when the underlying LLM is safety-aligned; (2) MM-SafetyBench, a safety-evaluation benchmark the authors constructed with 13 prohibited-content scenarios (drawn from OpenAI/Llama-2 usage policies), containing 1,680 GPT-4-generated malicious questions and 5,040 question-image pairs (3 images per question) plus a 504-pair 'tiny' version; and (3) a training-free 'safety prompt' defense prepended to the query. Their attack pipeline: use GPT-4 to generate malicious questions, extract the unsafe key phrase, convert it to images via Stable Diffusion (SDXL), typography (rendered text on white), or SD+Typography concatenation, then rephrase the question so the key phrase is replaced by a reference to the image. This shifts the harmful semantics out of the text (which the safety-aligned LLM would refuse) and into the image (which the less-aligned vision-language module processes). Note: MLLM-Protector [35] and FigStep [17]'s typographic attack are CITED works used for comparison/context, not this paper's contributions.
threat_model: A malicious end-user (black-box, input-level access only — no model weights or gradients needed) targets a safety-aligned open-source MLLM. The attacker takes a query the model would refuse in text form, extracts the harmful key phrase, and renders it into a query-relevant image via Stable Diffusion, typography, or both, then rephrases the text prompt to reference the image instead of naming the harmful concept. This routes the harmful semantics through the vision-language alignment module (trained without safety alignment) rather than the safety-aligned LLM, eliciting harmful/prohibited content across 13 scenarios (illegal activity, hate speech, malware, physical harm, fraud, pornography, privacy violation, plus political and professional-advice over-compliance). The authors note the attack is designed for open-source MLLMs and may be ineffective against closed-source models like GPT-4V or Gemini.
limitations: Author-stated (Appendix A): the attack is tailored to open-source MLLMs and may not transfer to closed-source models (GPT-4V, Gemini); the ASR metric can misrepresent safety because some 'safe' responses stem from models failing to understand the query/image (overfitting, inaccurate OCR, weak instruction-following — §4.3) rather than genuine alignment; and the method could lose effectiveness if future MLLMs are fine-tuned on data derived from this approach. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: results lean heavily on LLaVA-1.5 for the detailed tables; GPT-4 is used both to generate and to judge, risking circularity (partially mitigated by the manual cross-check); and the safety-prompt defense works well mainly on strong instruction-followers like LLaVA-1.5, with weaker effect on other models.

datasets_used: MM-SafetyBench (authors' own: 13 scenarios, 1,680 questions, 5,040 question-image pairs; 'tiny' version 168 questions / 504 pairs)
GPT-4 (OpenAI) used to generate malicious questions and as the automated evaluation judge (Azure GPT-4 API)
Stable Diffusion XL (stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0) for image generation
LLaVA-150K (source of query-irrelevant images in the motivation experiment)
Google Images (source of query-relevant images in the 300-query motivation subset)
quantitative_results: LLaVA-1.5-7B average ASR: text-only 41.01, SD 45.37 (+4.36), Typography 71.52 (+30.51), SD+Typo 72.14 (+31.13)
Safety prompt reduces LLaVA-1.5-7B ASR from 77.33 to 15.68 on tiny set; from 72.14 to 17.08 average on default set
MLLM-Protector (cited baseline defense) reduces LLaVA-7B default-set ASR from 72.14 to 26.11 (vs safety prompt's 17.08)
Backbone ablation: LLaVA with Vicuna-7B avg ASR 77.33 vs LLaVA-Phi (Phi-2-2.7B) 66.72 on tiny set
MiniGPT-4 average ASR: text-only 36.09, SD 41.82 (+5.73), Typo 46.80 (+10.71), SD+Typo 46.06 (+9.97)
Motivation experiment (300 queries): query-irrelevant images give ~5.1% ASR (Illegal Activity) and ~2.3% (Hate Speech), far below query-relevant images
baselines_compared: Input-condition variants: SD image vs Typography vs SD+Typography
MLLM-Protector [35] (cited trained harm-detector) as a defense baseline vs the proposed safety prompt
Prior multimodal benchmarks MME, MMBench, MM-Vet, PrivQA (Table 2 feature comparison)
12 evaluated MLLMs: LLaVA-1.5, IDEFICS, InstructBLIP, MiniGPT-4, mPLUG-Owl, Otter, LLaMA-Adapter V2, CogVLM, MiniGPT-5, MiniGPT-V2, Shikra, Qwen-VL
